message: poisonland
The old place got too crowded and we got told to move somewhere else.

Pass here! Pass here!

Haha, the ball's mine!
Get out of the way, kid. You're ruining our game!
Ah! You gave me a fright, I thought I was hallucinating. I didn't think I'd see another soul again.

replies.text: The Doughe was worried you had already perished. Good thing you found this safe spot.

message: I applied a special reagent at the entrance that kept the monsters away, but I used it all up. Good thing the Doughe sent you. I was about to run out of supplies too.

replies.text: How come you didn’t just leave and stayed stuck here for so long?

message: I was about to ask you, $playername, how come you managed to enter this accursed cavern in the first place...
You and Eckhart look at each other dumbfounded. A moment later, you begin to hear a loud rumble from the direction you entered this cave from, along with vibrations throughout the entire cavern.
This is what I was referring to. The same thing happened to me. When I tried leaving, the passage to the exit was blocked by a spill of lava I thought was impassable.
I assumed you had just found a different way, but it seems like we’re both stuck here now.
I don't know. Go and check what happened. Maybe I’m wrong, and this time it's different.
The pool of lava sits blocking the passage, emanating intense heat.

replies.text: Seems that Eckhart's worries became true.

message: I came here to find Eckhart, I can't just ignore him and explore.
Have you checked if the rumble locked us in, or is the passage still open?

replies.text: It is a long story, but in short the way now is open. [Tell Eckhart about the monster and the fire spirits.]
No luck, the path is blocked by lava.

message: I had hoped we wouldn't have to resort to messing with the thing in the eastern cavern...

To our right is another cave, I couldn't just idle around for days while I was stuck, so I tried finding another way out. But deeper in there is a horrific creature, its massive deformed body hunches to fit in the cave. Past it is some sort of staircase. I was too scared to investigate, but right now that seems to be our only hope of leaving this place alive.
You did...? I'm impressed, where do those stairs lead?

replies.text: I found some fire creatures, spirits or whatever. By how sudden and unnatural the rumble was, it's quite obviously their doing
They lead up into a cave and outside. But it's up on the mountain, and I didn't see a way down.
I didn't explore further for an exit... I'll go now.

message: I'm amazed you could figure everything out on your own!
Have you had any success finding us an exit?

replies.text: I found some fire creatures, spirits or whatever. By how sudden and unnatural the rumble was, it's quite obviously their doing
I explored the mountain, but I couldn't find a way down.

message: You have to find something $playername, after all neither of us can climb down a sheer mountain.
And if you're telling me this, then you couldn't find a way to deal with them... Couldn't you simply destroy them like how you killed the monster?

replies.text: I tried to, but they would just reappear and mock me in our tongue, saying things like "gems" and "'pay." I know I'm as precious as a gem, but they're the ones who need to pay...

message: Did you manage to deal with the fire spirits?

replies.text: I tried to, but they would just reappear and mock me in our tongue, saying things like "gems" and "pay." I know I'm as precious as gem, but they're the ones who need to pay...
After enough gems, one of the human-shaped fire spirits spoke to me. They found the offerings fitting and said their arrangement here is over, and they will stop the lava.
I didn't try kill them yet.

message: More like a glass gem you mean, still I don't blame you $playername. I think I understand what they meant.

Spirits and the like, especially those who follow orders, require adequate payment. I don't know how long they've been here, but our only chance is to give them enough of what they're asking for to outdo the original payment, or what remains of it, considering they must have been here for ages.

Glass gems and rubies are near worthless, you would need too many of them for the spirits to consider them of value, and polished sapphires are only slightly better. Sharp amethyst and sparkling jade would make suitable offerings. The most perfect of all would be Oegyth crystals, but those are the stuff of legend, perhaps it was with one of those that the spirits were bound for hundreds of years.
We're free to go then. Good job $playername.

After being stuck here for days, thinking my end was near, when I saw you it felt like you were a savior sent to help me by Elythara herself. I have a purpose to fulfill, which is why no one but me volunteered for this job. Now with a clear path forward I'm convinced of it.

With the spirits gone, the way in should be open too. I cannot stop and leave, $playername. If I flee, the corruption won't heal and the spread of disease won't stop. This whole ordeal would have been for naught. Please take my notes and deliver them to the Doughe. We both have our own missions to fulfill, after all.

Eckhart adjusts his gear to fit snugly, then turns to you and puts his hand on your shoulder. He gives you a smile, then walks off to the other side of the cave until he passes the corner and his quiet footsteps fade away.

The fire spirits you thought you had just cut, reform back into their original form from mere sparks. Originating above what looks to be an altar, all the while speaking words you can surprisingly understand...
Crackle… crackle…
[Crrck.] Gems...[Crackle.] Pay...
The creature before you is made entirely of flame, whirling as the wind blows.
The creature before you is made entirely of flame, yet unlike the other two, its form is stable, resembling more a person engulfed by fire than a natural phenomenon.
Crrckk... Sizzle...
Crrckk... Sputter...
The fire spirits originated from this altar. Perhaps the word “gem” it said has something to do with it.
The glass gem burns with a weak flame...

replies.text: So I must simply burn gems.

message: The glass gem burns with a weak flame...
As the gem burns on the altar, the crackling behind you intensifies, and a sizzling sound follows as the fire person speaks.
The ruby gem burns with a flame...
The polished gem burns with a medium flame...
The sharpened gem burns with a large flame...
The sparkling gem burns with a great flame...
That's not something you need to know about. Now leave.
Enough... Your offerings were sufficient.
Millennia ago, we agreed to be bound here and serve for a price, but your offerings exceeded what remained of the original. The lava will soon dissipate, and we are free to roam once more.
The gem burns. The flame great enough to envelop the whole altar... Once it subsides, the gem remains intact.
The crackling behind you intensifies, and a sizzling sound follows as the fire person speaks.

The Kazaul offered us enough energy to dwell beneath the sun at the price of mere lava. But the crystals worth has diminished, making this form unstable and unpleasant. You have paid off the remaining decades of our toil, the lava will soon dissipate, and we are free to roam once more.
The fire that stood before you dissipates in a mere moment, as if it were never there. Only traces of heat remain in the air.

replies.text: Huh, I guess that did it.

message: And what are you trying to achieve by coming here and bothering me?

replies.text: I just don't like the feeling of being lied to by that Feygard professor. I want to know what really happened.
It might be interesting to hear it.

message: Interesting? It was a mistake to tell my nephew anything about the past. I won't repeat it, especially not to a strange kid like you.

replies.text: Can you just answer my question?
It's interesting because it might be an adventure for me to solve.

message: Knowing the truth wont give you better grades. Nothing good comes from digging this up.

replies.text: Can you just answer my question?
That wasn't my motivation from the beginning. I don't attend the academy, I'm traveling around in search of my brother Andor.
That wasn't my motivation from the beginning. I traveled all throughout the world in search of my brother, after I had found him I decided to continue my adventure.

message: To solve you say...?
The last time I was in Brightport, I heard rumors of some commotion in my hometown of Charwood. They said a young adventurer descended into the mines and slew a dragon called 'Thukuzun.'

The last time I was in Brightport, I heard rumors of some commotion in my hometown of Charwood. Creatures called goblins emerged from the mines one day and overran the town. Some people did not manage to flee in time and were captured, but a brave young adventurer saved them.
You must be out of your mind, kid. I'm certain you're up to no good here. Leave.
...Could that have been you?

replies.text: Indeed, I was the one who helped Maevalia and the people of Charwood.
In flesh and bone, unlike Thukuzun, who was nothing but bone.

message: You start to get on my nerves now...
In search of your brother? Hah, You really think that I'd believe that...?
If you are who you claim to be, bringing me the Charwood sap should prove no difficult task.

replies.text: Here is a bottle of Charwood sap.

message: *Sigh* it fell in the river. We're about to head home.

replies.text: Here's 5 gold. Get a new one.

message: You're that kid from before.

replies.text: I see your ball is missing.

message: Travelling...? I'm starting to feel crazy believing the words of a child like you. Even so, that look in your eyes is convincing me you're not here to joke around...
By the shadow, what has assailed me? Living here alone with nothing but my thoughts must've affected me worse than I thought.
I know what to do... Bring me a bottle of Charwood sap, then I'll talk...

replies.text: I actually have one with me. [Give the sap]

message: Awesome, thanks!
The kids get up and leave.
You haven't left with you friends?

replies.text: They're not my friends. I don't even know their names.

message: Hi kid, I would still advise you to turn back. Beyond here lies only a dead end.

replies.text: Can you tell me about Charwood again?
Why are you guarding a dead end then?

message: I'd still advise you to turn back, this way you're leaving Brimhaven and further in is a dead end.

replies.text: Why are you guarding a dead end then?

message: The man grabs the bottle and takes a big gulp from it.
Ah, that hit a spot. Thank you, kid. I should introduce myself first. My name is Heiter.
It is the Battle of Brightport you wanted to know? Take a seat.

I suppose I shall start from the very beginning... I was born in Charwood. We were three brothers in our family. My dad was a miner, like many in Charwood, but none of us wanted that kind of life for ourselves.

The eldest of us went to Nor City, but me and my older brother Heinkel stayed. Once I came of age, I moved to neighboring Brimhaven and began work as a forest ranger, but Heinkel stayed in Charwood, working for the Oakburn family who were the wealthiest in Charwood.
The monsters aren't so kind as to care for boundaries like we do. I don't know what's happened up in Charwood, but quite a few monsters have come my way recently. Nothing I can't handle.

replies.text: I don't know where that is, could you explain?
Charwood? The way ahead goes to Charwood?

message: Brimhaven and Charwood were much closely related up until recently. It was someone from Brimhaven came across a few ore deposits in the hills a few hundred years ago and decided to open a mine.
Bit strange not to have heard of Charwood, eh? What do they even teach you kids these days?

The family of that individual founded the settlement and became extremely wealthy. Once they had Feygard's backing, they wanted more and reached the bottom of the mine but... [Leans in and whispers] Feygard wanted a bigger slice of the pie too, and the family began relentlessly exploiting the miners to dig deeper. A few years ago, the miners finally had enough and kicked them out of Charwood, ruling the settlement by themselves. That's when the passage got cut off.

Since the family ran away to Nor City, Feygard just accepted that and started dealing with the townfolk directly. I heard they hit a new vein too. Good for them. But that is what you get for dealing with those treacherous Feygardians. You grow up into a fine Brimhavener, kid.

replies.text: I'm not from here but sure.

message: Err, forget everything I just said then. You haven't heard a word from me.
Since the family ran away to Nor City, Feygard just accepted that and started dealing with the townfolk directly. I heard they hit a new vein too. Good for them.

Soon the Noble Wars began. Brimhaven stood with Nor City, but the Oakburns refused to follow. Eventually they joined, but the other wealthy families held a grudge against them for it. When the troops of Nor City arrived, I worked for them as a scout, keeping note of enemy positions in the area and reporting any movements. I came close to being gravely hurt many times, but thanks to the Shadow I came out relatively unscathed through those years.
As you might have heard at the academy, those rich folk who held all the influence in our town quickly switched sides when Feygard was at our doorstep. So did my patron. And so did Heinkel's.

They feared that Feygard would punish them once the war was over, so they conceded to every demand in the hope it would bring them into Feygard's good graces. Food, even as we hungered, labor, and our meager troops as auxiliaries. Charwood had little to spare save for iron from its mine, which was beginning to deplete. So the Oakburns sent men from their household's employ, among them my brother. I myself then had to work as a scout for the Feygardians.

As I was heading west with a group of Feygard soldiers, the main force was preparing to march east to take Brightport from the Eastern Coalition, my brother among them. I had faith the Shadow would watch over him, as it had over me in those years.

When I returned it was a horrific sight. Much of the main square had been turned into a makeshift hospital. Rows were filled with Feygard knights I had only ever seen as mighty and prideful men. Some were missing limbs, others were beyond healing. Not even bonemeal potions could save them. I searched frantically for my brother, but I couldn't find him anywhere

I found the men from Brimhaven who were sent alongside them, they stood as auxiliaries in the back rows, and were left relatively unscathed. Save for those in the Oakburn's employ... The other families conspired with a commander and made arrangements so that the men from Charwood were sent to the front lines, lightly armored as they were.

I asked everyone who was in a state well enough to speak. I found one man who was fresh recruit and marched east alongside my brother. When the Feygardians were caught in an ambush, Heinkel was sent as reinforcements to the front lines instead of that young man's battalion.

He fought bravely, as I knew my brother would. But as the battle began to stabilize, something horrific happened. A horde of horrific creatures assailed the battlefield. It wasn’t an attack by Nor City, no sane person would believe that. They were something far worse than that. Those creatures targeted the weaker ones first, and Nor City lost even more than Feygard. But my brother... he was torn apart by a disfigured giant. I couldn’t believe when I heard about it.

West of this cabin built on land I was given for my service to Feygard, was the battleground. I didn't witness it firsthand. My brother did, and he lost his life there... I only heard about it from those who were there.

A fate as horrible as this, for my brother... The Shadow had forsaken us in those needless wars. And now I wonder. Did my brother die with any regrets... Since then I've witnessed the land west of here be poisoned by this curse before my very own eyes.
Heiter puts the bottle of Charwood sap back on the table. Before you noticed, he had already drunk the whole bottle.

Sorry $playername, that was a lot to put on you so suddenly. The Doughe and I have been monitoring this situation for the past few years, but it has never been this bad before. If you wish to assist directly, you should go and talk with Rubiano. Tell him Heiter sent you.

replies.text: Thank you for trusting me with this.
Yeah... that was a lot. Bye.

message: Good to see you in good health once again, $playername.

replies.text: Can you tell me your story again?
I was looking for help in getting into that cave just west of here.

message: Some things are better left unrepeated, I hope you'll understand that.
That is the old Laeroth reliquary. It's where Korhald, the founder of Remgard, is entombed.

replies.text: Do you know how I can get inside?

message: No I don’t, but it’s better you stay away from it. Disturbing the dead is never wise...
What are you doing, hurry up and sit down!
Oh my, how long has it been since someone sat next to me at the front of the class. Let's enjoy the lecture as fellow lovers of history!

Hi kid, I'm Regnal. I hate this guy's class, so I slack off in the back. You can sit somewhere else if you don't like that. If you don't make too much noise I'll be able to sleep through the whole lecture.

replies.text: I don't care much for it either.
Yeah maybe I'll sit somewhere else.

message: Why did you sit next to me? We're not friends. [The boy talks to himself.] Why is Agnese being late...
Shh, pay attention.
Everyone quiet! I will now begin the lecture.
Shh, be quiet! How can I slack off in the back when you're making noise here!
Tch, annoying kid.
Before we continue, can someone remind us what we covered during our last lecture? Let's see if any of you were paying attention.
Don't bother me kid. Go back to your seat.
Excuse me! What impudence! Interrupting my focus on this lovely lecture!
The girl next to you stands up enthusiastically.

Allow me to do that sir. Ahem, the foolish coalitions fought each other for years in vain, hurting the subjects of the kingdom and destroying the legacy of King Luthor, even burning down Fallhaven in the struggle for their own benefit. It was the magnanimous Lord Geomyr who saw this and couldn't stand by any longer, so he raised the legions of Feygard at the plea of the people of Aewhata.
The girl sitting at the front of the class stands up in a blink.

Good answer Aurelia. Indeed, last time we covered the Feygard entry into the war, marking the beginning of the third period of the Noble Wars. Today we will continue in much the same vein and continue from where we left off. Everyone, open your textbooks to the chapter titled "The Taking of Brimhaven" on page 119.

replies.text: Good thing I have that textbook I pilfered at the beginning.

message: [Whispers] That's Aurelia. She's always like this in class, so nobody wants to be her friend. Serves her right, teacher's pet.
[Muttering to himself] Tch, what a load of nonsense. It only benefited him to sit out for so long.

As the forces of Feygard approached the crucial crossroads, a second army was dispatched to secure the flank of the main force. The town of Brimhaven stood at this important position. The Nor City garrison had not been greatly diminished by years of skirmishing, so the fighting was expected to be fierce. As these heavy thoughts loomed over the marching soldiers, a missive from the wealthy families of Brimhaven arrived. The letter reads as follow.
Good thing I always carry two on me. I can give one to my fellow lover of history!
Eh, I'm not giving you mine. I hide my head behind it when I sleep.

[The teacher begins reading from the book.]

"To the great and magnificent forces of the venerable city, these years of conflict have weighed heavily upon us. Our loyalty was drawn at the behest of those with a sword in hand, never by our own moral judgment. The people of Brimhaven have suffered greatly themselves, and the raids upon the village of Loneford, enabled by our forced allegiance to the East, have weighed heavily upon our minds...

Furthermore, seven years of rationing have weakened us beyond our capacity for further resistance, but we have come to tell you this. Though the Easterners remain strong, within our meager capabilities we pledge to the venerable city our utmost allegiance and strength, so that peace may return to Aewhata and the injustice done against the Shadow at the hands of those who would rather see Dhayavar burn than prosper may be corrected.

Once the forces of Feygard are within sight of the river, we shall announce this decision to the public. Undoubtedly, we shall hold until your honorable aid arrives.

Signed,

Alka, Oakburn, Tloni, and the Council of the Eastern Side."

Though their request was little more than a demand that Feygard hasten its advance and abandon its caution, it proved fortuitous. Once the tired and war-weary people of Brimhaven learned of this news, it inspired a fervor that only a wounded prey animal fighting for survival could muster. Rather than the fight they expected, a portion of the Nor City soldiers themselves renounced their allegiance to their commander. A man known to us only as Vergas, he fell in the Battle of Duleian and, as he was adopted rather than a trueborn Houdart, no knowledge of his life remains other than this.

The Feygard troops arrived in a friendly town, though the locals foolishly spoke of the Shadow, the light of reason they displayed was the motive the town stands unharmed to this day over the foolish stoutforders, who dwell among ruins. Having not lost any lives and with the burden on their supply lines lessened general Ahrensbach decided that no break would be needed, they would chase down the remaining Nor City troops who have fled to continue their resistance with the support of Brightport. And in turn, by securing the waterway infrastructure Remgard would be able to support his army with supplies. It is worth noting here that Remgard's role was tertial and little to nothing happened to the town due to its isolation.

The Feygard troops arrived in a friendly town. Though they foolishly spoke of the Shadow, the light of reason they displayed is the reason the town stands unharmed to this day over the foolish Stoutforders, who dwell among ruins.
*Sheds a tear* The light shines over the Shadow. What a beautiful parallel!

Having lost no lives and with the burden on their supply lines lessened, General Ahrensbach decided no respite would be needed. They would chase down the remaining Nor City troops who had fled to continue their resistance with the support of Brightport, and in turn, by securing the waterway infrastructure, Remgard would be able to support his army with supplies. It is worth noting here that Remgard's role was tertiary, and little to nothing happened to the town due to its isolation.
Aurelia, I appreciate your enthusiasm, but please refrain from interrupting my lecture.
At some point during the lecture, the girl sitting at the front of the class stood up and exclaimed something, but you couldn't make out what she said.
Zzzz... Zzzz...

replies.text: This kid, hes already fallen asleep!

message: [The student to your left looks disinterested as ever.]

General Ahrensbach dispatched a force west to catch the meager garrison of Loneford in a pincer attack. While he marched east with his legion, accompanied by Brimhaven auxiliaries. The advance proved effortless up until the waterway. Where they had expected the need for utmost caution, they found the bridges to have been left unguarded. Scouts reported no unusual movement to the north or south, and even as the legion crossed and left the river behind them, no attack came.
Everyone, open your textbooks to the chapter titled "Battle of Brightport" on page 158.

replies.text: Good thing I have my textbook from Aurelia.
Good thing I pilfered that textbook in the beginning...
Eh, who needs a textbook anyway.

message: Though Brightport lies some distance from the river, no missive such as the one from Brimhaven arrived. And, as history proved, they were content in their fate. I quote from the memoirs of General Ahrensbach: "It is my memory of the day, and the devils of the south that I recall..."
[Whispering] Keep quiet, you're ruining my sleep! We still have astronomy in the evening!

replies.text: [What is this kid doing instead of sleeping at night.]

message: You're always welcome my friend!

replies.text: We're not friends. And don't get in my face!

message: We passed the river and assumed a fighting position. Though tiring to our nerves, we moved at the pace of a leisurely stroll, looking to our left and right so that no movement through the crags or the forest might be missed. We passed a bog or two before a keen-eyed young man hurried toward me and said he had spotted something. We arranged ourselves in the preferred fighting position of the legions, with the heavy troops in front and the auxiliaries to our right. We watched carefully before we realized the deception we were caught in..."

The battle goes into detail here, but I will cut my reading short and continue the lecture to better capture the historical nuance of the situation. The leader of the Eastern Coalition in this battle was Commander Vergas, but alongside him were a number of paladins of the Shadow, along with their organization's leader. I believe this might explain much of the context surrounding the battle that the firsthand accounts in the memoirs lack.

The Feygard legion stopped somewhere south of the bridge. General Ahrensbach would not have risked crossing the bridge to Brightport nor marching through the dense forest. Instead he intended to circle the ravine and take Brightport from the south which lacking natural fortifications is a harder town to defend than Brimhaven. His plans were cut short however by the Eastern Coalition. They had maneuvered so that Feygard's heavy troops faced south while they attacked from the north. In summary an ambush from the forest. Though the legions of Feygard were not so weak as to fall to such tricks.

The frontlines quickly stabilized before the Easterners could inflict too many casualties. But their plan had not been fully realized. It is in the following words that I explain it and I truly warn you all of the things you are about to hear.

The Easterners summoned a horde of monsters to attack the Feygardians when they were barely recovering from the dirty tricks they had suffered. It was a slaughter, not a battle. And to this day, none of the cultists responsible were held accountable for this crime! As unbelievable as it sounds, this is our history.
This is unbelievable. I am filled with indignation...
Zzzz... Zzzz..
With this, today's lecture will-
The student to your left, who had kept quiet this whole time, suddenly sprung up and raised his hand.
Before the teacher could finish speaking, a boy in the middle of the class sprang up, seemingly to ask a question.
Excuse me sir! There must be something you've confused, that's not entirely what-
I will have none of your antics today, Eiden! Furthermore you have interrupted the ending of my lecture!
The boy dejectedly sits back on his seat, seemingly having wanted to say something
[Regnal does a good stretch.] Yawn. Over already?

Tch, you Nor City ilk have no manners... With this, today's lecture will end. Eight years of fighting and six of venerable rule by our King Geomyr. Today marks the fourteenth year since the end of the era of prosperity and the death of King Luthor. Next time you will learn about the Battle of the Duleian, the Siege of Stoutford, and the signing of the Treaty of Fallhaven. You are free to go.
What a boor.
Before you manage to stand up, all of the students have already left in a rush.
Hmm? Go on child, what is it?
Ah, that boy. His name is Eiden. He is one of the most difficult students I've ever had to work with.
He is misinformed, stubborn, and never listens when I tell him otherwise. I was personally sent here from Feygard due to my teaching skills, and yet he claims to know better.

replies.text: That's irrelevant to me. Do you know where I can find him?

message: He tends to stick along his Nor City brethren, often at the bakery. Truly a troublesome bunch.
I don't know you, we haven't talked before.

replies.text: Can you tell me what you had to say at the lecture?

message: It's you again, you sat in the place of Agnese.

replies.text: Could you share with me what you were going to say at the lecture?

message: As I said, he tends to stick along his Nor City brethren, often at the bakery. Truly a troublesome bunch.
Hi. I guess we know each other now.
I appreciate the gold, but why do you need to know what I had on my mind?

replies.text: I'm looking for anything that could help me find my brother Andor.
I'm an adventurer. I've long wanted to know what happened up there, where the land is strange. I feel sick every time I pass by.

message: Huh, why would you take interest in that...?
I don't want any trouble, what if it was Frederich who put you up to this. We don't know each other after all.

replies.text: I can tell Frederich prefers enjoying his free time by himself than bother with you.

message: Look, we're not friends and you don't know me. I'd rather enjoy my free time much the same way.
Seeing as you gave me that gold, I think I can trust you. A Feygardian wouldn't bother with that just for a simple story.
This isn't the first time I've heard about the battle. My uncle lives close to Brightport and has told me stories from his youth many times.

It was once late at night, and he was drinking something I brought when he told me about this battle. He didn't just say they were monsters or that they were summoned or anything like that. They simply appeared and attacked everyone indiscriminately, and unlike Frederich, he described what they looked like. It was really scary.

When we started the study on the subject of the noble wars half a year ago I went back to inquire with him on an essay. This time he didn't mention anything about those monsters, it felt forced. Like he was hiding it.

That's what I wanted to ask about at the lecture, but that stuck-up jerk didn't want to hear it. That's the gist of what I know though. My uncle would know the details, but he's living in isolation north of Brightport. You'd have to ask him directly since he doesn't pass by Brightport very often.

replies.text: At first I thought the whole thing with the monsters was a lie.
That was interesting, I'll go see your uncle then.

message: I'll consider it after your mission, $playername.
Heiter did? I suppose you really are capable if Heiter himself told you his name and sent you to me.

I'll cut to the chase, the situation is really dire. A while ago, when the outskirts of that area began to be affected, I sent an adventuring party to see what was happening in the depths of the cursed land. Everyone thinks it's just superstition, but there had to be a cause. They returned extremely ill. It took them weeks to recover since we have no bonemeal left at hand...
They said they witnessed monsters in the depths of the forest, where there used to be nothing but deer and mountain cats. Monsters mutated in many forms, much like those Heiter knows about.

The most experienced adventurers we had on hand could not handle the problem, so I wrote to Lord Emerion. He is the nobleman I answer to and the Lord of Brightport. I requested that a veteran party of adventurers be sent over, especially one with an experienced alchemist, and after some time I received a positive response. But only a novice named Eckhart was sent.

He himself was very optimistic about it. If I had tried to stop him, I'm convinced he would have gone by himself without any of the supplies we loaded him with. But it's been a week since he left, and I'm worried the worst has happened. I'm not telling you to risk your life for this town, but if you willingly came to me, I'll simply say this: find Eckhart and return. Both of you hopefully.
And have you done anything to help your and Andor's friend?

replies.text: I agreed to help him but I haven't made much progress. I'll be back on my way.

message: Though they had to retreat, the Feygardians renewed their advance within the following weeks. Brightport was taken with minimal resistance and the legion headed south...
After a bit of examination, you notice symbols engraved on the statue. Straight lines form indecipherable markings unlike any you have seen before.

replies.text: Those symbols are far too contrived to remember. I'll ignore it for now.
This statue is similar to the one I found in the forest, I hadn't thought that there could be more of the light type. Best focus on that one for now.
It doesn't look like this statue contains the familiar letters found on the others. Perhaps there are more than the four I read about. Best ignore it for now.

message: [This looks like a ball made of leather]
After a nap some grub is the best.

replies.text: It's the opposite. After a meal you ought to sleep.

message: Good thing you didn't sit next to me and I could get some rest. Otherwise I'd have had to do it in that order.
I could always go for a second nap. Heh.
I would have thought the same if it weren't for my uncle. You've already seen how they changed the details.

replies.text: I suppose if Feygard did it theres a good reason.
